bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: Union College founding professor and clergyman

burial.plot_notes: Vale Cemetery Schenectady NY per FA

education.notes: Per Yale bio reference: "the Rev. Dr. Andrew Yates (Yale 1794)."

career.notes: One of the first professors at Union College per Schenectady biographical records. "Well versed in ancient and modern languages including Holland Dutch."

cross_connections.display_label: Both connected to John Owen Dominis's Hawaiian royal orbit
Born in his Schenectady home on March 10, 1832

narrative_blocks.header: The Dominis family lived in his Schenectady home
narrative_blocks.body: The Schenectady Gazette (August 27, 1932) records that John Owen Dominis was 'born March 10, 1832 at 26 Front Street in the home of Reverend Dr. Andrew Yates' — the Dominis family boarded with Yates while the sea-captain father traded abroad, and the Dominis daughters who died at boarding school were buried in Christopher Yates's family plot at Vale Cemetery, where Yates and Hannah Allin Hooker are also interred. That boarder's son went on to marry Queen Liliʻuokalani, the last sovereign monarch of the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi, making a Schenectady parsonage the birthplace of the kingdom's final male consort.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandson served Farragut's staff at Mobile Bay
narrative_blocks.body: Andrew Yates's grandson, Captain Arthur Reed Yates (Naval Academy 1857) — son of his eldest son Reverend John Austin Yates — served on Admiral David Farragut's personal staff at the Battle of Mobile Bay on August 5, 1864, receiving Farragut's written commendation for gallantry and dying in November 1892 on the very eve of promotion to Commodore. At that same battle, Captain James Hooker Strong — whose mother Dolly Goodwin Hooker was the own sister of Yates's wife Hannah Allin Hooker — commanded USS Monongahela and rammed the Confederate ironclad CSS Tennessee, making two men bound through this Schenectady household heroes of the same decisive naval engagement.

narrative_blocks.header: One of Union College's founding professors in Schenectady
narrative_blocks.body: Reverend Andrew Yates was among the first professors at Union College in Schenectady — an institution chartered in 1795 whose founders included his cousin Joseph C. Yates, later Governor of New York (1823-1825). His command of ancient and modern languages, including Holland Dutch, made him one of the most learned academics in upstate New York of his generation, and he later served as principal of the Polytechnic Institute in Chittenango, New York.

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife was kin to Texas's founder
narrative_blocks.body: Andrew Yates's first wife Mary Austin (b.1776; d.1806) was described in a Schenectady biographical record as 'of English stock and a relative of the founder of Austin, Texas' — a reference to Stephen F. Austin (1793-1836), the American empresario whose colonization of Mexican Texas made 'Austin' a legendary name in the story of westward expansion. The precise kinship is not documented, but the community clearly recognized the connection, and Yates honored his first wife's memory by naming their daughter Mary Austin — a name that eventually passed to his granddaughter Mary Austin Yates Stuyvesant, buried at Saint Mark's-in-the-Bowery in New York City.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

cross_connections.display_label: His sisters' graves share her husband's burial plot at Vale

bio.bio_blurb: Hooker descendant; wife of Rev. Andrew Yates

burial.plot_notes: Vale Cemetery Schenectady NY per FA

narrative_blocks.header: Husband's ward became consort of Hawaii's last queen
narrative_blocks.body: The Dominis family — Captain John Dominis, wife Mary Jones, and their young son John Owen — boarded at 26 Front Street, the Yates home in Schenectady; John Owen was born there on March 10, 1832, and the Dominis daughters who died young were buried in the Yates family plot at Vale Cemetery alongside Hannah and her husband. That same boy went on to marry Queen Liliʻuokalani, the last sovereign monarch of the Kingdom of Hawaiʻi, becoming the kingdom's last and only male consort.

bio.bio_blurb: Hooker descendant who married into the Stuyvesants

burial.plot_notes: Saint Marks Church-in-the-Bowery Churchyard per FA

naming_inspiration.description: Named 'Mary Austin' after her father Rev. Andrew Yates's first wife Mary Austin (d.1806) — honoring his first family in the second.

narrative_blocks.header: Both Reade sisters married into the Hooker family
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Austin Yates married John Reade Stuyvesant in 1840 — whose father Nicholas William Stuyvesant had married Catherine Livingston Reade, the sister of Helen Sarah Reade who had married Mary's uncle James Hooker of Poughkeepsie (H00797). The Reade sisters thus each connected to the Hooker world: one by marrying James Hooker directly, the other through her son who married back into the Hooker line a generation later.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
FindAGrave memorial records

bio.bio_blurb: New York Stuyvesant who wed a Hooker descendant

burial.plot_notes: Saint Marks Church-in-the-Bowery Churchyard per FA

narrative_blocks.header: His second marriage merged Stuyvesant and Hooker blood
narrative_blocks.body: After his first wife died childless, John Reade Stuyvesant married Mary Austin Yates, a great-granddaughter of Thomas Hooker — and their children carried both the Stuyvesant and Hooker lines.

sources.label: FindAGrave memorial records

bio.bio_blurb: Her name joined the Hooker and Stuyvesant lines

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name cluster 'Hooker Stuyvesant' (in full birth name) honors maternal Hooker lineage and the Stuyvesant family through father John Reade Stuyvesant.

narrative_blocks.header: Her name carried both Hooker and Stuyvesant lines
narrative_blocks.body: Helen Mary Hooker Stuyvesant was named to hold two dynasties at once — daughter of a Stuyvesant father and a Hooker-descended mother, born at Hyde Park on the Hudson.

sources.label: FindAGrave memorial records

burial.plot_notes: Saint James Episcopal Churchyard Hyde Park NY per FA

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Livingston' honors the Livingston family through her grandmother Catherine Livingston Reade (wife of Nicholas William Stuyvesant X02831) and great-great-grandmother Margaret Livingston Stuyvesant.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale-educated husband died in New Mexico at forty-seven
narrative_blocks.body: Katharine Livingston Stuyvesant married Francis Reed Butler, a Yale-educated lawyer who practiced in New York, Chicago, Minneapolis, and finally New Mexico, where he died of pneumonia in Raton on January 28, 1890 at age forty-seven. Katharine died sixteen months later in Poughkeepsie, and Butler's Yale obituary confirmed they left no children.

narrative_blocks.header: Named her adopted daughter Hannah for her grandmother
narrative_blocks.body: Ann Eliza Yates Ireland and her husband John R. Ireland adopted a child they named Hannah — the same first name as Ann's grandmother Hannah Allin Hooker, daughter of Captain James Hooker of Windsor. The name carried the Hooker-Yates heritage forward even into an adoptive relationship.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

naming_inspiration.description: First name 'Thomas' likely honors Rev. Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford CT.
Middle name 'Hooker' continues the Hooker family name.

narrative_blocks.header: Named Thomas Hooker; died at eleven months old
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker Yates was named in full for Reverend Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford and the progenitor of the Hooker line — an extraordinary naming act in the Yates household six generations from that same Thomas Hooker. He died on August 5, 1827, eleven months after his birth, never knowing the weight of the name he carried.

burialCemetery.notes: Rev. Andrew Yates and Hannah Allin Hooker Yates buried here.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
